front: People: militaristic East Slavic frontier horsemen of the steppe, many living along the Don River, who served the Polish and Russian armies in exchange for political autonomy
work: General / Giveaway Identifiers

front: People: governed themselves through community councils called rada, which elected chieftains called hetmans and other officers called atamans
work: General / Giveaway Identifiers

front: People: a papal diplomat reported that they never ate bread and that their name derived from a term for the comparative agility of goats
work: General / Giveaway Identifiers

front: People: their highest symbol of power was a gem-encrusted mace called the bulawa
work: General / Giveaway Identifiers

front: People: an 18th-century manuscript called the "Eyewitness Chronicle" records their history
work: General / Giveaway Identifiers

front: People: historian Shane O'Rourke has argued that certain 20th-century ethnic-assimilation measures directed against them could constitute genocide
work: General / Giveaway Identifiers

front: Institution: proto-state founded on the island of Khortytsia, giving rise to a self-governing polity headquartered in the "land beyond the rapids" and led by chief officers called koshovyi
work: The Zaporozhian Sich and the Cossack Hetmanate

front: Event: a demand to increase the number of "registered" Cossacks helped spark this affair (involving Daniel Czaplinski) and the resulting 1648 uprising against the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth
work: The Zaporozhian Sich and the Cossack Hetmanate

front: Event: during the Khmelnytsky Uprising, the Cossacks sought a "three-nation" Commonwealth but instead pledged allegiance to Tsar Alexis of Russia at this council
work: The Zaporozhian Sich and the Cossack Hetmanate

front: Document: an unamended treaty that would have elevated some Cossacks to the nobility and expanded their registry
work: The Zaporozhian Sich and the Cossack Hetmanate

front: Document: a 1710 constitution drafted at Bendery by hetman Pylyp Orlyk, guaranteed by the exiled Swedish king Charles XII, that created a legislative General Council and anticipated Montesquieu's separation of powers
work: The Zaporozhian Sich and the Cossack Hetmanate

front: Event: at this 1709 battle, Cossack hetman Ivan Mazepa betrayed Peter the Great for Swedish king Charles XII
work: The Zaporozhian Sich and the Cossack Hetmanate

front: Ruler: Crimean khan who deserted a Cossack leader three times, eventually forcing him to allow the annexation of the "left-bank" region of modern-day Ukraine
work: The Zaporozhian Sich and the Cossack Hetmanate

front: Figure: founder of a Cossack subgroup; a fellow Cossack was hetman Pylyp Orlyk
work: The Zaporozhian Sich and the Cossack Hetmanate

front: Painting: Ilya Repin's 1891 work showing Cossacks laughing while composing a mocking reply refusing Sultan Mehmed IV's demand for surrender, legendarily including the line "could not slay a hedgehog with his naked arse"
back: The Reply of the Zaporozhian Cossacks to Sultan Mehmed IV (Ilya Repin)
work: Depictions in Russian Art: Ilya Repin's "Reply of the Zaporozhian Cossacks"

front: Painting: for this painting, the Grand Chamberlain of a court refused to pose, so Repin secretly sketched the back of his bald head, and a gypsum mask was used to capture the likeness of an ill former student
back: The Reply of the Zaporozhian Cossacks (Ilya Repin)
work: Depictions in Russian Art: Ilya Repin's "Reply of the Zaporozhian Cossacks"

front: Painting: shows Cossacks packed into a boat called a chaika during a storm
back: Cossacks on the Black Sea (Ilya Repin)
work: Depictions in Russian Art: Ilya Repin's "Reply of the Zaporozhian Cossacks"

front: Painting: late in his life, Ilya Repin depicted an elaborately dressed Cossack in this work
work: Depictions in Russian Art: Ilya Repin's "Reply of the Zaporozhian Cossacks"

front: Painting: Bogdan Willewalde painted Cossacks greeting children in this town
work: Depictions in Russian Art: Ilya Repin's "Reply of the Zaporozhian Cossacks"

front: Painting: shows three dejected men with red-plumed helmets being captured by Cossacks
work: Depictions in Russian Art: Ilya Repin's "Reply of the Zaporozhian Cossacks"

front: Novel: Leo Tolstoy work whose protagonist Olenin fights in the Caucasian War, befriends the old hunter Eroshka, and pursues a romance with Maryanka
work: Literary Depictions

front: Novel: in this Tolstoy work, Olenin's marriage plans are complicated when his rival Luka is shot in the belly during a raid
work: Literary Depictions

front: Novel: Mikhail Sholokhov work whose protagonist saves a boy from drowning and impresses others by shooting pheasants on the wing
back: And Quiet Flows the Don (Mikhail Sholokhov)
work: Literary Depictions

front: Novel: in And Quiet Flows the Don, the protagonist falls in love with this woman but whips her when he learns of her affair with Eugene
work: Literary Depictions

front: Novella: Nikolai Gogol work whose title Cossack character is tied to a tree and burned alive at the story's end
work: Literary Depictions

front: Poem: Anna Akhmatova work that includes the line "silent flows the river Don," possibly referencing Cossack folk songs
work: Literary Depictions

front: Figure: German officer who organized a volunteer Cossack cavalry force that was incorporated into the Waffen-SS against his will in February 1945
work: World War II and the Repatriation Question

front: Event: many Cossacks defected from the Soviet Union to serve in these units of the German army during World War II
work: World War II and the Repatriation Question

front: Event: operation under which as many as a million POWs, including Cossacks, were forcibly repatriated to the Soviet Union after being handed over by the British per terms agreed at Yalta
work: World War II and the Repatriation Question

front: Event: postwar Soviet policy under which officials in Cossack khutor and stanitsa communities were ordered to extirpate them as an independent group, since their Zaporozhian and Kuban branches had sided with the White Army
work: The Russian Civil War and Decossackization

front: Event: Cossack revolt under Yemelyan Pugachev, whose Yaik Host took Kazan from Catherine the Great
work: Revolts Against the Tsars

front: Event: Cossack uprising led by Stenka Razin during the reign of Tsar Alexius
work: Revolts Against the Tsars

front: Event: Don Cossack uprising against Peter the Great, who suppressed it by killing many of them
work: Revolts Against the Tsars

front: Institution: elite brigade of the late Qajar dynasty in Iran, named for and modeled on the Cossacks, mobilized by officer Reza Khan for the 1921 coup that overthrew the Qajars
work: Beyond Russia: The Persian Cossack Brigade and Cultural Legacy

front: Composer: incorporated the folk-song form called the dumka, popularized by Cossack-descended composer Mykola Lysenko, into his chamber works
work: Beyond Russia: The Persian Cossack Brigade and Cultural Legacy

front: Work: Franz Liszt tone poem, with sections named for a "ride" and "march," based on a piano etude named for Cossack leader Ivan Mazeppa
work: Beyond Russia: The Persian Cossack Brigade and Cultural Legacy

front: Event: New Zealand PM who aired a TV commercial featuring historical Cossack figures (including Ivan Mazeppa and Yemelyan Pugachev) dancing to warn that the Labour government was communist
work: Beyond Russia: The Persian Cossack Brigade and Cultural Legacy
